topic: A person discovers their partner has been taking secret phone calls
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment was a physical weight, pressing down on Clara’s chest until she couldn't draw a full breath. She was staring at the phone screen, a jagged shard of evidence mocking her composure. The call log was a tapestry of late nights and unfamiliar numbers, a secret life she had never known existed. Her hands were trembling so violently she had to grip the edge of the kitchen counter to steady them. When Liam walked in, whistling a tune, she didn't look up. The sound of his casual presence felt like an intrusion, a spotlight illuminating the catastrophic unraveling happening inside her. "Liam," she managed, the word coming out thin and reedy. Her voice sounded alien to her own ears. She pushed the phone across the table, the small gesture feeling monumental, a desperate attempt to externalize the internal earthquake.

[story 2]

Mark paced the length of the living room, his movements jerky and uncoordinated. Every floorboard creaked under his frantic tread, amplifying the roaring in his ears. He kept replaying the snippets of conversation he’d overheard—low, urgent tones that didn't belong to their shared reality. His jaw was clenched so tight it ached, and he kept running a hand through his already disheveled hair, messing it up further. When Sarah finally confronted him, her eyes wide and glistening, Mark simply recoiled, stumbling back against the sofa. "What are you talking about?" he choked out, his voice rough with disbelief and a rising tide of panic. He felt a strange, hot pressure building behind his eyes, a desperate need to flee the room, to escape the suffocating atmosphere of the revelation.

[story 3]

Eleanor sat rigidly on the edge of the bed, her knuckles white where she gripped the duvet. The world seemed to have tilted on its axis; the familiar patterns of the room—the way the light fell, the placement of the books—had dissolved into a blurry, terrifying abstraction. She kept blinking rapidly, trying to clear the sudden, overwhelming influx of sensory data. When David finally admitted it, his voice a strained whisper, Eleanor couldn't process the words. Her breath hitched, a shallow, panicked intake of air. She stood up abruptly, needing space, needing distance from the undeniable truth laid bare before her. Her legs felt weak, unsteady, as if gravity itself had momentarily abandoned her.
